skill.name: developing-genkit-dart
skill.description: Helps developers build AI agents, flows, tools, schemas, and model integrations with the Genkit Dart SDK.
skill.summary: Build Genkit Dart agents, flows, tools, schemas, and plugin integrations.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: Most static command-execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline backticks, Dart examples, and shell commands shown as documentation. The audit confirms one risky installation pattern that pipes a downloaded script into bash, plus legitimate examples for environment API keys, local HTTP endpoints, MCP subprocesses, and filesystem-capable agent tools.

medium_findings.title: Downloaded Script Piped To Bash
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends installing the Genkit CLI with curl piped directly into bash. This is a real supply-chain risk because downloaded shell content executes without review or pinning, but the context is installation documentation rather than evidence of malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present in an installation block. Confidence is high for the dangerous pattern, while intent appears instructional rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem-Capable Agent Middleware
medium_findings.description: The middleware documentation shows tools that can list, read, write, and replace files in a configured workspace. This is legitimate Genkit functionality but requires strict root directory boundaries and approval controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access capabilities and approval middleware are explicitly documented. The risk is contextual because the examples describe a restricted root and approval workflow.

medium_findings.title: MCP Filesystem Server Execution Example
medium_findings.description: The MCP reference launches a filesystem MCP server through npx and exposes its tools to model calls. This can be safe in a controlled workspace but can expand agent access if users copy it without sandboxing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly start an external MCP filesystem package and allow model tool discovery. The documentation does not show malicious use, so the risk remains elevated but not blocking.

low_findings.title: API Keys Read From Environment In Examples
low_findings.description: The Anthropic and OpenAI references show API keys loaded from local environment variables. This is a standard secret-handling pattern and no evidence shows the keys are logged or sent to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code passes environment values into provider initialization. I found no evidence of credential exfiltration in these lines.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many static command findings come from Markdown code fences, inline Dart identifiers, schema class names, and ordinary code examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these locations are Markdown or Dart documentation, not executable Ruby code. This explains the large volume of static false positives.

low_findings.title: Localhost And Provider URLs In Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are local development endpoints, a package page, or an OpenAI-compatible provider base URL. I found no evidence of hidden collection endpoints or unauthorized outbound transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and match documented development or provider examples. The risk is low unless a user reuses localhost examples in production.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer is piped directly to bash. Users should prefer a package-manager install or inspect and pin downloaded scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command contains a direct network download into bash. This is a well-known dangerous installation pattern even when the source is intended to be legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent File Write Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The filesystem middleware includes write_file and search_and_replace tools. These should be scoped to a restricted root and guarded with approval for destructive edits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool list explicitly includes file write and replacement capabilities. The documentation also mentions a restricted root, which reduces but does not remove risk.

content.value_statement: Dart developers need accurate examples for Genkit flows, tools, schemas, and provider plugins. This skill supplies focused Genkit Dart guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Genkit Dart initialization, generation, streaming, embeddings, tools, and flows.
Provides plugin guidance for Google AI, Firebase AI, Anthropic, OpenAI-compatible providers, Chrome, Shelf, middleware, and MCP.
Shows how to define typed schemas with schemantic for tools, flows, and structured outputs.
Guides local Genkit CLI usage for development UI, tracing, and flow testing.
Documents remote flow clients, Shelf HTTP integration, and MCP host, client, and server patterns.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not include a runnable sample application.
Provider credentials, model access, and billing must be configured outside the skill.
Some examples use local development URLs that must be changed before deployment.
Filesystem and MCP examples require careful sandboxing and user approval controls.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Genkit Dart Flow
use_cases.description: Create a typed flow that calls an LLM, validates inputs, and can be tested with the Genkit CLI.

use_cases.title: Add Provider Plugins
use_cases.description: Configure Google AI, Firebase AI, Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq, or local Chrome model integrations for a Genkit app.

use_cases.title: Expose Agent Tools Safely
use_cases.description: Use middleware, MCP, and Shelf references to connect tools, HTTP endpoints, and approval workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Genkit Dart project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a simple Genkit Dart flow that accepts a text prompt, calls Gemini, and returns a short response.

prompt_templates.title: Add Typed Tool Input
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Genkit Dart tool with schemantic input and output types for this task: [describe task]. Include validation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate a Provider Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to configure Genkit Dart with [Google AI, Firebase AI, Anthropic, OpenAI, or Groq] for this application context.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Agent Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of flows, tools, MCP, and deployment boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Genkit Dart agent design for flow structure, tool permissions, schema safety, provider setup, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: User asks for a Genkit Dart flow that summarizes customer support messages.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines a flow name, input schema, model choice, prompt structure, validation notes, and local testing steps.

output_examples.input: User asks how to connect Anthropic Claude to a Dart AI agent.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the plugin reference, environment variable setup, model selection, and where to place provider configuration.

output_examples.input: User asks whether an agent should use filesystem middleware.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes restricted root directories, read-only defaults, approval for writes, and testing with non-sensitive files first.

best_practices: Use schemantic schemas for tool inputs, flow inputs, and structured model outputs.
Run dart analyze and test flows with the Genkit CLI before sharing final code.
Restrict filesystem, MCP, and remote flow access to the smallest required scope.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode provider API keys in Dart source files or examples.
Do not copy localhost remote-flow URLs into production configuration.
Do not enable file write tools without root-directory limits and approval checks.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Genkit Dart flows, tools, schemas, provider integrations, middleware, MCP connections, and HTTP flow endpoints.

faq.question: Does it support Flutter applications?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance targets Dart and can support Flutter apps that call Genkit flows or use compatible client patterns.

faq.question: Which model providers are covered?
faq.answer: It covers Google AI, Firebase AI, Anthropic Claude, OpenAI-compatible providers, Groq-style endpoints, and Chrome local Prompt API usage.

faq.question: Does it manage API keys?
faq.answer: No. It shows environment variable patterns, but users must provision, store, rotate, and protect their own provider credentials.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is mostly documentation, but it includes a pipe-to-shell installer and examples for filesystem and MCP agent tools.

faq.question: Can it deploy Genkit services?
faq.answer: No. It documents development patterns and HTTP integration, but deployment configuration must be handled by the user project.
